output:
# ISOFUSE / FULCRUM: Per-Graph Locality-Conditional Local-Global Fusion for Graph-Level Anomaly Detection

A new research idea generated from the provided Introduction on graph-level anomaly
detection (GLAD). The core move: a glocal GLAD detector that combines a whole-graph
(global) anomaly score and a node/substructure (local) anomaly score does so with ONE
global fusion weight applied identically to every graph. We name this degeneracy ISOFUSE
and propose FULCRUM, which replaces the single fusion weight with a structured, monotone,
partially-pooled map of an observable per-graph anomaly-locality covariate, while provably
collapsing to the original detector at its default setting.

Notation is ASCII throughout: beta (fusion weight), theta (map parameters), tau (pooling
strength), d_G (per-graph covariate), s_glob / s_loc (global / local anomaly scores),
"->" (maps to), "<=" / ">=" (inequalities), "approx" (approximately).

---

## Section 1. Background and Current State of the Art

### 1.1 The graph-level anomaly detection problem

Graph-level anomaly detection (GLAD) aims to identify whole graphs that are significantly
different from the majority of a graph population. As the Introduction notes, a few
anomalies can cause large losses, so GLAD matters across biochemistry (abnormal proteins),
neuroscience (brain-network disorders), and online platforms (fraud rings) [9,10,12,13].
The dominant setting is unsupervised or one-class: a detector is trained on a corpus
assumed to be mostly normal, learns a model of normality, and at test time emits a single
real-valued anomaly score per graph; graphs are then ranked and evaluated, almost
universally, by pooled Area Under the ROC Curve (AUROC) and sometimes Average Precision
(AP) over a held-out set in which the anomaly class is a small minority [1,2,3,4,5,9,10].

GLAD inherits two foundations. From deep anomaly detection it inherits one-class objectives
and reconstruction/distillation residuals as normality signals: Deep SVDD [19] and DeepSAD
[20] cast anomaly scoring as distance to a learned normal description; the broader
robustness literature established that learned representations have brittle, off-manifold
regions [21]. From graph representation learning it inherits message-passing backbones --
GCN [15] and GIN [14] -- and graph-pooling/readout operators that compress node embeddings
into a graph embedding, including differentiable hierarchical pooling (DiffPool [16]),
self-attention pooling (SAGPool [18]), and multiset pooling (GMT [17]); classical graph
kernels (Weisfeiler-Lehman [31]) provide a non-neural baseline. Benchmarks are drawn mostly
from molecular and social graph-classification collections repurposed for GLAD: TUDataset
[22], MoleculeNet/Tox21 [24], and OGB [23], with PyGOD [25] standardizing outlier-detection
tooling.

### 1.2 Two normality signals: GLOBAL and LOCAL, and their fusion

A recurring structural insight across the GLAD literature is that anomaly evidence lives at
TWO scales. A graph can be anomalous GLOBALLY -- its whole-graph embedding, overall
topology, size, or density is atypical -- or LOCALLY -- a small substructure, motif, or a
handful of nodes/neighborhoods is atypical while the rest of the graph is normal
[1,3,4,8,9,10]. Classic graph-based anomaly detection already distinguished structural from
local pattern deviations [12,13].

Because either scale can carry the signal, the strongest modern detectors FUSE both:

- GLocalKD [1] (the "glocal" detector, our primary host) trains a predictor GNN to
  distill the representations of a fixed, randomly initialized target GNN at both the GRAPH
  level and the NODE level. The anomaly score is the sum of a graph-level distillation
  error and the (mean) node-level distillation error. The two terms enter with EQUAL
  weight; in our notation this is a fused score s(G) = s_glob(G) + beta * s_loc(G) with
  the global fusion weight pinned to beta = 1.

- HimNet [3] (our second host) builds HIERARCHICAL memory: a node-level memory module and
  a graph-level memory module, each yielding a memory-based reconstruction error; the final
  score combines node-level and graph-level errors through a global weighting.

- iGAD [8] is a (supervised, class-imbalanced) dual-discriminative detector that jointly
  exploits substructure (local) and node-feature (global) signals, combined through learned
  global parameters.

- OCGTL [2] ("raising the bar") and GLAM [5] are strong one-class / unsupervised-GNN
  detectors; SIGNET [6] adds self-interpretability; GOOD-D [7] targets graph
  out-of-distribution detection. BWGNN [11] rethinks the spectral filter used for anomaly
  detection. These define the competitive SOTA frontier.

In every fused detector, the local-vs-global balance is set ONCE, globally: an equal-weight
sum [1], a tuned scalar hyperparameter [3], or a learned-but-shared combination [8]. The
fusion weight is treated as a fixed architectural constant of the model, not as something
that could vary per graph.

### 1.3 The metric and its known confounds

GLAD is judged by pooled AUROC on a single small test set per dataset. Zhao and Akoglu [4]
delivered a sobering analysis: graph-level outlier-detection benchmarks are heavily
CONFOUNDED by trivial properties -- above all graph SIZE -- so that pooled AUROC can reward
detectors that merely separate graphs by node count, and a single pooled number per dataset
hides what a detector is actually keying on. This is the field's clearest published warning
that the standard evaluation instrument averages away structure; yet the response has been
better protocols and decorrelation, not a re-examination of the fusion weight itself.

### 1.4 Partial pooling and stratified evaluation

Two mature toolkits, so far unused for the fusion weight, are central to our proposal. (a)
Empirical-Bayes / hierarchical partial pooling -- James-Stein shrinkage [28], Efron-Morris
[26], and multilevel models [27] -- estimates many related quantities by shrinking noisy
per-unit estimates toward a shared prior, with data-poor units shrunk more. (b)
Subpopulation / stratified evaluation -- Group DRO [29], behavioral and slice-based testing
[30] -- measures performance on strata rather than a single pooled average, exposing
opposite-signed errors that cancel in the mean.

### 1.5 The reframing

Putting these together yields the reframing that motivates this proposal. The local-vs-global
fusion weight in a glocal GLAD detector is a SINGLE GLOBAL SCALAR. Anomaly LOCALITY,
however, is HETEROGENEOUS across a graph population: some anomalous graphs are locally
anomalous (a rogue substructure) and some are globally anomalous (a holistic topology shift).
A constant fusion weight is a compromise operating point on the local-global axis. Its damage
is averaged away by pooled AUROC -- the very metric already known to be confounded [4] -- and
the raw materials needed to do better (the model's own per-node deviation field, the graph's
observable structure) are already computed but never repurposed to STEER the fusion. That is
the gap ISOFUSE names and FULCRUM closes.

---

## Section 2. Key Findings and Analysis

### 2.1 ISOFUSE formalized (the representational impossibility)

Let a glocal detector produce, for graph G, a global anomaly score s_glob(G) and a local
anomaly score s_loc(G), fused as

    s(G) = s_glob(G) + beta * s_loc(G),    beta = beta_global (one constant for all G).

For GLocalKD [1], s_glob is the graph-level distillation error, s_loc is the mean node-level
distillation error, and beta_global = 1. For HimNet [3], s_glob and s_loc are the graph-level
and node-level memory errors and beta_global is a tuned constant.

Define the per-graph optimal fusion weight beta*(G) as the weight that maximizes that graph's
anomaly-vs-normal separability (operationalized in Section 3 and grounded with ground truth on
semi-synthetic data). When anomaly locality varies across G, beta*(G) is a NON-CONSTANT
function of an observable locality property: locally anomalous graphs concentrate their
signal in a few large node-level residuals, so a larger beta (upweight s_loc) ranks them
correctly; globally anomalous graphs carry their signal in s_glob, and a large beta only
injects noisy node residuals that DILUTE the global signal, so a smaller beta is optimal. A
single constant beta_global cannot equal a non-constant beta*( . ): it is REPRESENTATIONALLY
INCAPABLE of expressing the per-graph optimum. The best global beta is a compromise that is
simultaneously too small for the locally anomalous tail and too large for the globally
anomalous tail.

### 2.2 Why the harm is SILENT, SCALES, and VANISHES

SILENT under the pooled metric. AUROC is a ranking integral over the whole test set, in
which anomalies are a minority. Under-scoring local-anomaly graphs (beta too small for them)
and over-diluting global-anomaly graphs (beta too large for them) produce OPPOSITE-SIGNED
ranking errors that partially cancel in the pooled ROC; the residual net effect on a single
pooled number is small and looks like a flat, healthy optimum. The size-confounding already
documented by [4] further camouflages the misallocation. The pooled metric is structurally
blind to a per-graph cancellation -- exactly the regime where stratified evaluation [29,30]
is required.

SCALES with heterogeneity. The damage grows with the SPREAD of anomaly locality in the test
population (the fraction of graphs whose beta*(G) departs from beta_global, and how far). A
population mixing local and global anomalies maximizes the gap.

VANISHES under homogeneity. If every anomalous graph shares the same locality, then
beta*(G) = beta_const for all G and a single global weight is optimal; FULCRUM should provide
no benefit. This vanishing condition is a falsifiable prediction (Section 3, T1), not a
loophole.

### 2.3 The observable, leakage-free covariate d_G

FULCRUM conditions the fusion weight on an observable per-graph covariate d_G that proxies
anomaly LOCALITY, computed WITHOUT anomaly labels (unsupervised GLAD has none at train time)
and without the student's fitted residuals on the test graph (to avoid feedback):

- Primary axis: the CONCENTRATION of the FROZEN target/teacher network's node-embedding
  deviation field. With t_v the frozen random-target node embedding (label-free, student-free,
  a fixed structural fingerprint already computed by GLocalKD [1]), set
  d_G = Gini( { || t_v - mean_u t_u || }_v )  (equivalently a normalized coefficient of
  variation or top-k energy fraction). High concentration -> a few structurally outlying
  nodes -> locality-prone; low concentration -> diffuse -> holistic.
- Secondary axes (multi-axis, O4): graph size n_G (directly addressing the size-confounding
  of [4]), degree-variance, and a spectral dispersion term; combined with the primary axis
  into a single rank-normalized index fit in-fold.

Leakage discipline (O3): the entire pipeline -- normalization percentiles, anchor d0, bin
edges, pooling strength, and theta -- is fit strictly on NORMAL training graphs inside each
cross-validation fold and FROZEN before any test graph is scored. A student-error-dispersion
variant of d_G is offered only as an ablation, under cross-fitting.

### 2.4 FULCRUM: the structured map and the exact-collapse identity

Replace the scalar with an anchored, monotone, partially-pooled map:

    beta(d_G) = clamp_{[beta_min, beta_max]}( beta_global + g_theta(d_G - d0) ),
    g_theta(u) = sum_k theta_k ( phi_k(u) - phi_k(0) ),   phi_k monotone non-decreasing,
    theta_k >= 0,   anchor d0 = median locality.

Because every term is anchored at phi_k(0) and there is NO surviving constant offset, theta = 0
makes g_theta(u) = 0 for ALL u. With theta_k >= 0 the map is monotone non-decreasing in d_G:
g_theta(u) < 0 below the anchor (downweight s_loc on diffuse/holistic graphs) and > 0 above it
(upweight s_loc on concentrated/local graphs). The monotone DIRECTION is a tested inductive
bias (reversed/non-monotone ablation A6), not an assumption.

Partial pooling. The shipped DEFAULT is a single shared map (one theta) -- the fully-pooled
limit. OPTIONALLY, per-locality-bin offsets delta_k ~ N(0, tau^2) over K bins are estimated by
empirical Bayes [26,27,28]; data-poor bins shrink toward beta_global; tau^2 is set by marginal
maximum likelihood. The unpooled free-per-graph estimator is the no-pooling limit (overfit
control); FULCRUM is the partially-pooled interpolation.

EXACT COLLAPSE (the do-no-harm guarantee). At theta = 0 AND clamp inactive
(beta_min < beta_global < beta_max), beta(d_G) = beta_global for every G, so

    s_FULCRUM(G) = s_glob(G) + beta_global * s_loc(G) = s_host(G)   for every graph G,

hence the per-graph scores are identical, the induced RANKING is identical, and therefore
pooled AUROC and AP are identical. The collapse is gated in CI as a value identity over all
graphs:

    E_collapse = max_G | s_FULCRUM(G) - s_host(G) | <= 1e-6.

Nested recovery: setting beta_global to the host's published value (1 for GLocalKD [1]'s
equal-weight sum; HimNet [3]'s tuned weight) recovers that detector exactly. The theta = 0
code path may dispatch literally to the host scorer, making the identity exact by
construction (no floating-point drift).

### 2.5 The C1-C4 wedge and the near-miss families

FULCRUM occupies a four-clause white space:

- C1 (layer + cost): operates ONLY at the score-fusion layer; reuses signals the host
  already computes (s_glob, s_loc, frozen-teacher embeddings); adds one O(n) per-graph
  covariate pre-pass and ZERO model retraining, zero added inference model.
- C2 (covariate): conditions on an OBSERVABLE, leakage-free per-graph locality covariate d_G.
- C3 (structure): a structured MONOTONE, hierarchically PARTIALLY-POOLED map -- neither a
  single global scalar nor an unpooled free per-graph weight.
- C4 (collapse): a PROVABLE exact collapse to the host's global-fusion detector,
  E_collapse <= 1e-6.

No prior line covers more than two of C1-C4. Six near-miss families, each missing at least
two clauses:

- NM1 (PRIMARY collapse target): glocal / hierarchical fusion detectors themselves --
  GLocalKD [1] (equal-weight beta = 1) and HimNet [3]. They have the fusion layer (C1) but
  fix one global weight: miss C2, C3, C4. GLocalKD's equal-weight sum is the degenerate
  beta = 1 special case FULCRUM generalizes.
- NM2: single-score one-class detectors -- OCGTL [2], GLAM [5], Deep SVDD/DeepSAD [19,20].
  They emit one score with no explicit local-global fusion axis: miss C1, C2, C3, C4 (no
  fusion weight to condition).
- NM3: supervised dual-signal detectors -- iGAD [8]. It combines substructure and node
  signals but is supervised, uses a learned global combination, conditions on no observable
  covariate, and has no exact-collapse identity: miss C2, C3, C4.
- NM4: learned graph-pooling / attention readouts -- DiffPool [16], SAGPool [18], GMT [17].
  They learn per-node weights as free end-to-end parameters over node FEATURES (not over
  local-vs-global SCORES), with no observable locality covariate and no collapse identity:
  miss C2, C3, C4.
- NM5 (SHARPEST on the covariate spirit): size-aware / debiased GLAD evaluation --
  Zhao-Akoglu [4]. It recognizes that an observable structural covariate (size) confounds
  GLAD and corrects for it, sharing the spirit of C2; but it DEBIASES or re-normalizes a
  single score rather than conditioning a local-global FUSION weight, is not a partially-pooled
  monotone map (miss C3), and has no exact-collapse-to-host identity (miss C4); covers about
  C2, misses C1, C3, C4.
- NM6: multi-scale / multi-view self-supervised GLAD -- SIGNET [6], GOOD-D [7], and the
  hierarchical/spectral lines [3,11]. They combine scales or views with global weights and no
  covariate-conditional, exact-collapsing map: miss C2, C3, C4.

Barrier (why the white space stayed open). The local-vs-global fusion weight was treated as a
fixed architectural constant -- most starkly an equal-weight sum [1] -- never as a per-graph
field. The model's own per-node deviation dispersion (a cheap, label-free signal already in
hand) was used to COMPUTE the local score but never repurposed as the fusion-SELECTION
covariate. The single pooled AUROC, already known to be size-confounded [4], mathematically
hides the per-graph cancellation. And the triple constraint that FULCRUM meets at once --
locality-tail lift, homogeneous-population no-regression, and provable exact collapse -- was
never co-targeted.

### 2.6 Obstacles O1-O6 (binding obstacle: metric blindness)

- O1 (entangled optimum): beta*(G) is confounded with size, density, and class label;
  locality correlates with the very size axis [4] warns about -> residualize the covariate on
  size/density and report size-decorrelated results.
- O2 (data poverty): GLAD test sets are tiny and anomalies are a minority, so per-graph or
  per-bin beta estimates are noisy -> partial pooling [26,27,28] is mandatory, not optional.
- O3 (observability + leakage-freeness): unsupervised GLAD provides no anomaly labels; the
  covariate must come from the frozen teacher and structure only, fit in-fold and frozen
  before scoring.
- O4 (multi-axis locality): locality is not one number (node-error concentration vs structural
  dispersion vs size) -> a fitted multi-axis index, ablated.
- O5 (simultaneous lift + exact collapse + valid score): the anchored no-offset map delivers
  lift while preserving an exact, CI-gated identity to the host at default.
- O6 (BINDING -- metric blindness): pooled AUROC averages away the opposite-signed mis-ranking
  of local- vs global-anomaly graphs, and the size confound [4] hides what remains; without
  locality-stratified, size-decorrelated evaluation plus the homogeneous-null, monotone-dose,
  and covariate-scramble falsifiers, the gap is undetectable. This is exactly why the field,
  reporting one pooled number per dataset, has not noticed the degeneracy.

---

## Section 3. Novel Research Idea (the 5q)

**Here is a high-level summarized insight of a research field Machine Learning.**

Graph-level anomaly detectors that fuse a whole-graph (global) anomaly score with a
node/substructure (local) anomaly score combine them with a single global fusion weight
applied identically to every graph. Because anomaly locality is heterogeneous across a graph
population, this constant weight is a representationally inadequate compromise whose damage is
hidden by the pooled, size-confounded AUROC that the field reports. Promoting the weight to a
structured, monotone, partially-pooled map of an observable per-graph locality covariate --
with a provable exact collapse to the original detector at its default -- converts a hidden
degeneracy into measurable, safely deployable gains.

**Here are the five core questions:**

**[Question 1] - What is the problem?**

Can the single global fusion weight that glocal graph-level anomaly detectors use to combine a
whole-graph (global) anomaly score and a node/substructure (local) anomaly score identically
for every graph be replaced by a per-graph, locality-conditional fusion weight -- a structured,
monotone, partially-pooled map of an observable, leakage-free per-graph anomaly-locality
covariate -- that improves detection on locality-heterogeneous graph populations while provably
collapsing to the original global-fusion detector at its default setting?

**[Question 2] - Why is it interesting and important?**

The implications are broad because the local-global fusion weight is a tiny, ubiquitous, and
universally overlooked component of modern GLAD.

P1 -- It exposes a hidden representational degeneracy in deployed detectors. Glocal and
hierarchical detectors [1,3,8] are among the strongest GLAD methods, yet their fusion weight is
a fixed constant (an equal-weight sum in the most-cited case [1]). Showing that this constant is
the degenerate special case of a map -- and that the gap it leaves is real but invisible to
pooled AUROC -- reframes a "solved" design choice as an open axis of improvement.

P2 -- It targets the safety-critical tail directly. GLAD is consumed in fraud, abnormal-protein,
and brain-disorder settings [9,10,12,13] where the COST of missing a particular anomaly is high
and heterogeneous. Locality-stratified evaluation surfaces which anomalies (localized vs
holistic) a detector reliably catches -- information a single pooled number erases -- and FULCRUM
improves the previously under-served tail rather than the easy middle.

P3 -- It is immediately deployable with a do-no-harm guarantee. Because FULCRUM collapses exactly
to the host detector at theta = 0 (E_collapse <= 1e-6), it can be shipped as a shadow overlay on
GLocalKD [1] or HimNet [3] with zero regression risk at default and benefits gated behind
validation. This lowers the barrier to adoption that pure-accuracy methods face.

P4 -- It is host-agnostic and reusable. The recipe -- promote a global fusion/blend weight to a
monotone partially-pooled map of an observable covariate, with exact collapse -- applies wherever
a detector additively combines local and global evidence, including iGAD-style supervised fusion
[8] and multi-view self-supervised detectors [6,7].

P5 -- It operationalizes the field's own warning. Zhao-Akoglu [4] showed pooled GLAD metrics are
size-confounded; FULCRUM turns that warning into method by (a) using size as one covariate axis
and (b) mandating size-decorrelated, locality-stratified reporting. A paper that does this will
push GLAD evaluation toward stratified, confound-aware practice (as Group DRO [29] and behavioral
testing [30] did elsewhere), influencing how the next generation of detectors is measured, not
just built.

**[Question 3] - Why is it hard?**

The difficulty is not implementing a weighted sum; it is making the per-graph weight LEARNABLE,
SAFE, and MEASURABLE under the exact conditions GLAD imposes.

Naive approaches fail concretely. (1) Tuning a single better global beta cannot help on a
locality-heterogeneous population, because no constant equals a non-constant beta*( . ); a global
sweep just relocates the compromise (this is precisely what falsifier T1 holds FULCRUM to). (2)
Fitting a free per-graph beta is hopeless: GLAD test sets are tiny and unlabeled, so an unpooled
per-graph weight has no signal to estimate from and overfits catastrophically (obstacle O2). (3)
Learning the weight end-to-end as attention over node features (DiffPool/SAGPool/GMT
[16,17,18]) conditions on the wrong thing (node features, not local-vs-global score reliability),
loses the exact-collapse safety net, and re-opens the leakage and overfitting problems.

The binding obstacles. (O6, binding) METRIC BLINDNESS: the standard pooled AUROC mathematically
cancels the opposite-signed mis-ranking of local- and global-anomaly graphs, and the size
confound [4] hides the remainder, so the very instrument used to judge GLAD cannot see the gap;
without locality-stratified, size-decorrelated evaluation and dedicated falsifiers the effect is
undetectable and unpublishable. (O3) LEAKAGE-FREENESS: the conditioning covariate must be
computable with NO anomaly labels and without feeding the test graph's own fitted residuals back
into its score, or the gains are an artifact; this forces a frozen-teacher/structural covariate
fit strictly in-fold. (O1) ENTANGLEMENT: locality is confounded with size, density, and class, so
a covariate that merely tracks size would reproduce the confound [4] rather than fix it. (O2) DATA
POVERTY: reliable per-locality estimates require empirical-Bayes partial pooling [26,27,28], whose
shrinkage strength must itself be estimated from scarce data. (O5) THE TRIPLE CONSTRAINT:
delivering lift while GUARANTEEING an exact, CI-gated identity to the host at default and keeping
the score a valid detector requires the anchored, no-offset map construction -- a stricter
demand than "just add a weight."

**[Question 4] - Why hasn't it been solved before?**

Prior work clusters into six families, each missing at least two of the four clauses C1
(fusion-layer-only, zero added training), C2 (observable leakage-free locality covariate), C3
(monotone partially-pooled map), C4 (provable exact collapse). No prior line covers more than
two of C1-C4.

- NM1 (the host detectors, primary collapse target): GLocalKD [1] and HimNet [3] fuse local and
  global signals (C1) but with one global weight -- GLocalKD's is literally an equal-weight sum
  (beta = 1). They miss C2, C3, C4.
- NM2: one-class single-score detectors OCGTL [2], GLAM [5], Deep SVDD / DeepSAD [19,20] have no
  explicit fusion weight to condition; they miss C1, C2, C3, C4.
- NM3: the supervised dual-discriminative iGAD [8] combines substructure and node signals but is
  label-supervised, uses a shared learned combination, conditions on no observable covariate, and
  has no exact-collapse identity; it misses C2, C3, C4.
- NM4: learned pooling/attention readouts DiffPool [16], SAGPool [18], GMT [17] weight NODE
  FEATURES with free end-to-end parameters, not local-vs-global SCORES by an observable covariate,
  and offer no collapse identity; they miss C2, C3, C4.
- NM5 (sharpest on the covariate spirit): size-aware / debiased GLAD evaluation, Zhao-Akoglu [4],
  conditions on an observable structural covariate (C2 in spirit) but debiases a single score
  rather than a FUSION weight, is not a partially-pooled monotone map (miss C3), and has no
  exact-collapse-to-host identity (miss C4); it misses C1, C3, C4.
- NM6: multi-scale / multi-view self-supervised detectors SIGNET [6], GOOD-D [7], and spectral
  rethinking BWGNN [11] combine scales/views with global weights and no covariate-conditional,
  exact-collapsing map; they miss C2, C3, C4.

The barriers that kept this open. (a) The fusion weight was framed as a fixed architectural
constant; the most influential detector simply SUMS the two errors [1], so there was no scalar
that anyone thought to vary. (b) The locality signal needed to steer fusion -- the dispersion of
the model's own per-node deviations -- was already computed to FORM the local score, but was never
re-read as a SELECTION covariate. (c) Pooled AUROC, the sole reported number and already known to
be size-confounded [4], hides the per-graph cancellation, so there was no measured symptom to
chase. (d) The combination FULCRUM targets -- lift PLUS exact collapse PLUS leakage-free
conditioning -- was never pursued jointly; accuracy-first methods abandon the collapse safety net,
and evaluation-critique work [4] stops at debiasing a single score. FULCRUM differs by binding all
four clauses together: a leakage-free, monotone, partially-pooled fusion map with a provable
identity to the host.

**[Question 5] - What are the key components of my approach and results?**

Method. Host a glocal detector H with global and local scores s_glob, s_loc and global fusion
weight beta_global (PRIMARY host: GLocalKD [1], beta_global = 1; SECOND host: HimNet [3]).
Replace the constant with

    s_FULCRUM(G) = s_glob(G) + beta(d_G) * s_loc(G),
    beta(d_G) = clamp_{[beta_min,beta_max]}( beta_global + g_theta(d_G - d0) ),
    g_theta(u) = sum_k theta_k ( phi_k(u) - phi_k(0) ),  phi_k monotone non-decreasing,
    theta_k >= 0,  d0 = median locality,

so theta = 0 gives beta(d_G) = beta_global for all G. The covariate d_G (Section 2.3) is the
rank-normalized fusion of frozen-teacher node-embedding-deviation concentration (Gini),
graph size n_G, and degree/spectral dispersion, fit in-fold and frozen before scoring.
Default = single shared map; optional empirical-Bayes partial pooling [26,27,28] over K
locality bins (offsets delta_k ~ N(0, tau^2), tau^2 by marginal max-likelihood). Monotone
direction is a tested inductive bias (A6).

Datasets. (1) Real GLAD benchmarks under the Zhao-Akoglu protocol [4]: TUDataset graphs [22]
repurposed for GLAD (PROTEINS, DD, NCI1, AIDS, MUTAG, ENZYMES, BZR, COX2, DHFR; social
IMDB-BINARY, REDDIT-BINARY, COLLAB), MoleculeNet/Tox21 [24], and an OGB molecular set [23],
tooled with PyGOD [25]. (2) SEMI-SYNTHETIC controlled-locality data: starting from a normal
graph family, inject LOCAL anomalies (plant a rare motif/clique/dense subgraph touching a few
nodes) versus GLOBAL anomalies (shift global density, size, or degree distribution / community
structure), with KNOWN per-graph locality and a tunable locality SPREAD. This yields a
ground-truth per-graph optimal beta and the monotone-dose axis.

Baselines. B1 local-only score; B2 global-only score; B3 host at default global beta
(GLocalKD equal-weight [1]) = PRIMARY collapse target / identity guard; B4 host with
best-global-beta swept on validation = RETUNED-global, the honest competitor used by the
homogeneous-null falsifier; B5 unpooled free per-graph beta = overfit control; B6
covariate-scrambled FULCRUM = placebo; B7 per-graph ORACLE beta (semi-synthetic) = upper
bound; B8 FULCRUM (single shared map); B9 FULCRUM + EB partial pooling. External SOTA shown
for context only (not collapse targets): OCGTL [2], GLAM [5], SIGNET [6], iGAD [8], HimNet [3].

Metrics. PRIMARY: locality-stratified AUROC/AP -- detection on the locality-heterogeneous
regime, and on semi-synthetic data the per-graph optimal-beta recovery error plus
locality-stratified AUROC and monotone-dose response. SECONDARY: pooled AUROC/AP NO-regression
versus host B3 (pre-registered margin = bootstrap 95% CI or 1.0 absolute AUROC point,
whichever larger); locality-stratified AUROC slope; SIZE-DECORRELATED AUROC per [4] (stratify
/ residualize on size to show the gain is not size-confounding); covariate calibration (does
d_G predict locality?); and the guardrail E_collapse <= 1e-6.

Ablations A1-A7: A1 covariate choice (teacher-dispersion vs structural vs student-error with
cross-fitting); A2 monotone basis family; A3 number of bins K; A4 pooling strength tau; A5
clamp band [beta_min, beta_max]; A6 map DIRECTION (monotone-increasing vs reversed vs
non-monotone); A7 host (GLocalKD [1] vs HimNet [3]).

Falsifiers. T1 HOMOGENEOUS-NULL: on a population whose anomalies all share one locality,
FULCRUM must NOT beat the RETUNED best-global baseline B4 (distinct from the theta = 0 identity
guard, which holds on ANY data); this isolates locality-conditioning from mere re-tuning. T2
MONOTONE-DOSE: FULCRUM's gain over B4 must grow with the injected locality spread. T3
COVARIATE-SCRAMBLE: permuting d_G across graphs (B6) must erase the gain.

Hypotheses (numeric ranges are [conjectured, no preliminary results]). H1: on
locality-heterogeneous benchmarks FULCRUM lifts locality-tail AUROC by approx +2 to +8 points
with pooled AUROC within the no-regression margin. H2: locality-stratified AUROC reveals
opposite-signed errors that the pooled number hides (a Simpson-like reversal between strata).
H3: EB partial pooling cuts per-bin beta variance approx 30-60% versus the unpooled control B5.
H4: covariate-scramble T3 erases >= 80% of the gain. H5: the gain grows monotonically with
locality spread (T2). H6: E_collapse <= 1e-6 and exact host recovery are verifiable by
construction.

Expected outcomes. FULCRUM should (i) match the host EXACTLY at default (B3 identity,
E_collapse <= 1e-6); (ii) improve the locality-heterogeneous tail without pooled regression and
without size-confounding [4]; (iii) approach the oracle B7 as partial pooling sharpens; (iv)
fail T1 on homogeneous populations and T3 under scramble, confirming the gain comes from
genuine locality-conditioning rather than re-tuning or capacity. The broader deliverable is a
reusable template -- promote a global local-global fusion weight to a monotone, partially-pooled
map of an observable covariate with provable collapse -- plus a locality-stratified,
size-decorrelated GLAD evaluation protocol.

Responsible AI and Data-Governance Considerations. F1 (do-no-harm default): the shipped default
is theta = 0 = exact host (shadow-deployable, always recoverable); any active configuration is
gated behind no-regression validation, and in high-stakes GLAD (fraud, clinical) no active
config ships until it clears no-regression on protected slices. F2 (fairness / covariate
governance): d_G must not become a proxy for a sensitive attribute -- in social-network fraud,
graph size or structure can correlate with community or demographic -- so we audit the covariate,
report detection disaggregated by group, and verify that locality-conditioning does not raise the
false-positive rate for any subpopulation. F3 (auditability): per-graph logging of d_G and
beta(d_G), an explicit collapse/disable switch, and human review for high-stakes flags. F4
(scientific integrity): all numeric ranges are marked conjectured; we release the E_collapse unit
test, the semi-synthetic locality generators and seeds, and the locality-stratified,
size-decorrelated metric code; scope is honest -- FULCRUM improves glocal-fusion GLAD relative to
its own global-beta default and a retuned-global baseline, not a universal-SOTA claim. F5
(dual-use / contestability): anomaly flags that affect people (fraud accounts, abnormal-protein
or brain-disorder calls [9,12]) require human-in-the-loop and an appeal path; the system never
auto-actions on a single score.

---
